# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Exploring Japanese films off the beaten path

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I've seen my fair share of Japanese film but I feel like everything I've seen is the obvious stuff. I've seen a bunch of Kurosawa's stuff, I've seen a few Godzilla titles, Ghost in the Shell, Akira, a bunch more anime

And that's all great but I'm interested in seeing something new that's off the beaten path. Any suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mind Game (2004) is wild. It’s an anime film that’s visually insane and emotionally intense, definitely off the beaten path.
2. One cut of the dead

Don't read anything about it. It's amazing
3. Funeral Parade of Roses

Memories of Matsuko

Heart and Yummie

Woman in the Dunes

Being Two Isn't Easy

Our Little Sister

Galaxy Express 999 (1979)

Angel's Egg

The Dagger of Kamui

Imprint

Mononoke the Movie: Phantom in the Rain

Tampopo

The Legend of the Stardust Brothers

Kamikaze Girls
4. Check out Drive My Car. Its long but I really enjoyed it and the length actually didnt bother me. It was quite engrossing actually and has a beautiful soundtrack.

Edit: by no means is this off the beaten path as it was the frontrunner nominee for best international feature at the Oscars recently.
5. House (1977)
6. Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes (2020) and River (2023)


You will thank me later
7. Tampopo
8. Kore-eda Hirokazu is my favourite filmmaker in the whole world. Anything by him. The modern day Ozu.
9. **Director:** Takashi Miike
10. Nobody's mentioned *Kwaidan* yet?!

## 2. ‘Road House’, ‘They Live’, ‘Escape from New York’, I feel like I’ve hit a theme, how should I continue?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Thoroughly enjoyed all three of these which form a sort of trilogy of men with mullets kicking ass!

I’d like to continue, either very much in the same vein, or maybe loosely continuing the theme to have it merge into something else?

Either way, these three films have a vibe!

So what’s next?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Trouble In Little China
  - Ah yes! Definitely! That’s the quadrilogy right there. Should have included that one.
2. Con Air?
3. Hard Target has one of the greatest mullets ever committed to film and JCVD kicks lots of ass while rocking a black leather duster. John Woo directs. Highly recommend.
4. Blood Sport, Tremors, Big Trouble in Little China, and the best ass-kicking mullet of them all, HARD TARGET
5. First Blood (1982)
6. Mad Max movies

Escape from LA
7. Brian Bosworth in Stone Cold
8. Escape from LA, The Thing
9. Cobra for sure

## 3. Southern gothic movies/shows

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Looking for more movies or shows that feel like True Detective,  Sinners, The Beguiled, Skeleton Key, Hatchet.. etc etc. just movies with a strong southern gothic feel

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Night of the Hunter
2. Angel Heart (1987) film
3. True Blood
  - The opening credits are the definition of Southern gothic to me
4. Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil
5. Sharp Objects
6. Southern Comfort
7. Black Snake Moan
8. Lovecraft County might hit that spot as well
9. American Gothic tv series

## 4. looking for a good movie to watch with my mom- something interesting and not too violent

**Score:** 19
**Body:** my mom wants us to watch a movie together, and the movie theatre in my town has nothing good to watch right now. any suggestions on movies we can watch at home?(preferably on netflix) please don’t suggest anything with too violent or sexual scenes(a little is fine, but not any more)

i’m not sure about her specific taste but something with an old fashioned vibe could maybe be nice

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Throw Momma from the Train (1987)
2. Nonnas with Vince Vaughn
3. The Princess Bride

The correct answer to what movie should I watch is always The Princess Bride.
4. Knives Out is a good one, my mum and I liked watching that together. But “our” movie that we rewatch together every year is Amadeus :)
5. Moonstruck
6. Catch me if you can
7. The Knives Out movies were fun. There was another train mystery movie that was awesome, but I forgot the name. Someone in here's, got it, I'm sure.
8. My Big Fat Greek Wedding

No violence, practically zero swearing, no overt sexuality (I think you see a couple of male characters shirtless). And if you know even a little bit about Greek-American culture, you’ll find it absolutely funny-because-it’s-true.
9. Fried Green Tomatoes
10. The Holdovers. Especially at this time of year.

## 5. Favourite slice of life?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Ny definition of slice of life is a movie that 

- has no obvious plot

- while there is a main character, there is no actual enemy (antagonist)

- realistic story (no unrealistic love stories, fight scenes)

- likely to be considered boring but actually beautiful characters, story, cinematography etc

Examples of movies I would categorise as such would be;

- The remains of the day (1993)

- La Dolce Vita (1960)

- Call me by your name (2017)

- The father  (2020)

- The son (2022)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lots of Linklater and Jarmusch films.
2. Perfect Days 

Train Dreams
3. In the Mood for Love (2000)
4. I think Lost In Translation fits this pretty well.
5. The Worst Person in the World
6. The Florida Project
7. inside llewyn davis
8. Columbus
9. Dazed and Confused fits all of these except for I have never heard anyone call it boring nor "beautiful characters" lol
10. The "Before Trilogy" with Ethan Hawke and Julie Deply.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Wake Up Dead Man is better than Glass Onion

**Score:** 2114
**Body:** I know this is probably gonna get me jumped in the comments, but after sitting with both movies, I honestly think Wake Up Dead Man clears Glass Onion.

Glass Onion is slick, funny, and very self-aware but it leans a little too hard on the wink-at-the-audience energy. The mystery almost feels secondary to the satire, and once you clock what it’s doing, the rewatch value kind of drops. It’s entertaining, but it feels lightweight in a way that never fully lands emotionally.

Wake Up Dead Man, on the other hand, feels meaner, darker, and more confident in its tone. The mystery actually breathes. The stakes feel real, the characters feel less like punchlines, and the movie trusts the audience instead of constantly reminding us how clever it’s being.

I’m not saying Glass Onion is bad (it’s not), but Wake Up Dead Man feels like the stronger film overall: tighter tension, better atmosphere, and a mystery that actually sticks with you instead of just getting applause in the moment.

Curious if anyone else feels this way or if I’m completely off base. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Glass Onion had a great ensemble but I agree it was more focused with being a satire than a murder mystery.


Wake Up Dead Man's main issue is some of the supporting cast are clearly there just to make up the numbers in terms of suspects and by about the halfway point you realise that it can probably only be one of two people who is guilty.


However I think it was a brave choice to just spend the first section of the movie focusing on Josh O'Connor's Father Jud and not have Benoit show up until a third of the way in.


It allowed them to more heavily explore the themes of modern day Christianity in America and how Johnson feels that divisive gatekeeping is diluting Jesus's message with the whole clash between Jud and Msgr Wicks.
  - I honestly kinda forgot what I was watching until Blanc showed up in the church.
  - This was the first movie I saw with Josh O’Connor and I’m really looking forward to more! He was great!
2. Much better than glass onion, but can’t beat knives out
3. I agree. 

The actual crime/mystery is Wake Up Deadman was ..fine. It's was perfectly fine. Not as good as the first, certainly better than the 2nd. 

The movie and themes itself tho? Wake Up Deadman is phenomenal. 

Benoit Blanc is funnier in Glass Onion I think. His big "explain the mystery" in that one is so funny. He doesn't really get that in the new one (for good reason).
  - the big moment atop the pulpit had me screaming, so funny!
    - When he interrupted everyone with The Phantom of the Opera I lost it
  - I mean, we got the pulpit scene and the “closed door murder” rant which honestly satisfied that itch.
4. I have yet to see anyone say otherwise. Honestly, the response to WUDM has just made it clear that people like Glass Onion way less than I thought
5. Yes. I love the performance of Josh O'Connor and Craig is as funny as ever.

I hope they keeps pumping out more Knives Out stories. Classic whodunit is always welcome in my book.

## 2. Airheads. Yay or nay?

**Score:** 1225

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yah!
  - Alright what’s your name

My names Pip

The band… the band name!
2. Buscemi is especially unhinged in this.
  - Buscemi is a Natural Treasure. 

He should be the 5th head on Mt. Rushmore.
3. I aint fartin on no snare drum!!
4. Lemmy or God?
  - Trick question: Lemmy IS God.
5. Trick question dipshit, Airheads is Yay
6. Dog Day Afternoon meets Wayne's World. What's not to love.
7. I always loved the ending when they're playing at the prison and Bucemi is thrusting into his bass along with the song and he looks at the audience and there's a couple of black guys staring at him intensely. Bucemi stops thrusting and calms down. Shit is fucking hilarious.

## 3. ​Why haven't Christian Bale and Christopher Nolan worked together on any film projects since the conclusion of The Dark Knight Trilogy?

**Score:** 816
**Body:** Christian Bale and Christopher Nolan have not collaborated on a film together since the conclusion of the Dark Knight trilogy. Is Bale no longer on Nolan's radar when it comes to casting choices? Does Nolan no longer view him as a leading man or a supporting character? What could be the reasons? 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Probably something very boring like schedule conflicts.
  - Also no law that says if you make 4 films you have to Tim Burton the shit out of a relationship
    - You won’t believe who is playing Depp’s romantic lead in the next Tim Burton movie.
      - Christian Bale?
        - Kirsten bell?
2. Probably a mix of never the right place or right time or Nolan doesn't think he has a role for him or Bale doesn't think there's a role for him
3. Nolan normally swaps out his male protagonist leads
  - Like the trick in the Prestige?
4. He also never worked with DiCaprio, Jackman, Chastain or McConaughey again. The lad's trying to mix things up.
  - Guy Pearce, Al Pacino, Robin Williams while he was still with us. Very consistent. Thus Christian Bale in this context is actually the special exception with Batman trilogy plus The Prestige.

## 4. RIP Rob Reiner

**Score:** 582
**Body:** Thank you for all the good times you have given your fans RIP

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Misery ; A Few Good Men ; The Princess Bride ; Spinal Tap ; The Sure Thing ; Stand by Me ; When Harry Met Sally..  - Though he seemed to lose that Midas touch in recent decades, his best works in the 80s and 90s defined his craft and created of generation of fans who fell in love with going to the movies.

RIP
  - Left us a great body of work
  - >The Princess Bride

Easily one of my top ten movies.  RIP
2. Man really put together a good Legal Drama, a great Rockmockumentary, and one of the greatest comedic romances of all time. Such an icon
  - Yes and was a memorable sitcom character Meathead
3. He always seemed to be smiling and in a good mood! Poor guy. So sad, and his wife too.
4. Say what now? How tragic!!!
  - [removed]
5. This is fucking tragic.  And his poor wife.  Jesus christ.
6. May he rest in peace🕊️

## 5. One of the greatest runs a director could ever ask for, absolutely devastating, RIP to Rob Reiner and his wife

**Score:** 447

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. RIP GOAT remember him in Princess Bride 🐐
2. An 11 out of 10 director.
3. Literally **no other director** has output verified classics across so many genres of film with that consistency.

Just from those 5:

✅ Kids adventure
✅ Horror/thriller
✅ Fantasy/comedy
✅ Romcom
✅ War drama
  - Mockumentary with spinal tap as well. I would put that under a different category to fantasy comedy cause that void is filled with princess bride
4. I never realised he directed Misery. Huge loss.
  - Same. Misery is a fantastic film. Will be hard to watch again now after this awful event.
5. This Is Spinal Tap, too. 

Iconic.
6. Such an awful tragedy. Rob Reiner made some wonderful movies, and generally seemed like a decent human being. RIP

https://people.com/rob-reiner-wife-michele-were-killed-by-son-sources-11868856
7. Where the hell is Spinal Tap?

Also, a pretty good actor. He was hilarious on The New Girl.
8. Om Shanti from India. Cannot forget "A few good men" and the incredible (11/10) "This is Spinal Tap". So sad reading about the way he and his wife had died.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Rob Reiner, 1947-2025

**Score:** 541
**Body:** Sadly, Rob Reiner and his wife Michele were found dead today and their deaths are currently being [investigated as a possible homicide](https://www.nbclosangeles.com/investigations/2-found-dead-at-brentwood-mansion-owned-by-director-rob-reiner/3815886/).

Let's remember Rob Reiner the filmmaker, the man who directed some of the funniest and most beloved movies of the 1980s. *This Is Spinal Tap*, *Stand by Me*, *The Princess Bride*, *When Harry Met Sally...* He definitely carved out a place in film history.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yeah honestly his 7 picture run of movies that were both popular and good from '84 to '92 was one of the best since William Wyler and has likely been unmatched since, I think he will be remembered as one of the greats of that era and may he and his wife rest in peace
  - So impressive those were his first 7 movies too.
  - Everyone mentioning that 7 picture run made me look up what the 8th movie was.
  - I know North got lambasted hard when it came out, and he did followup with The American President (which married him with an Aaron Sorkin script). But it wasn't to last and he just fell off after that. The only project of his that I have some interest in is Spinal Tap II, and I hope that turns out great.

As for North, it was based on a book from the 80s (and the script was co-written by the same author), and was loaded down with cameos, and may seem a bit convoluted in places.

But two things:

Firstly, this movie ain't Movie 43 levels of shit. It's got some entertainment value to it.

Secondly, Reiner still defended this movie as far back as 2016. Usually, a director would look at the worst they've done and possibly denounce them or clarify their mindset in producing them. But despite Reiner still thinking fondly of this movie, I don't disagree with him on it.

Overall, I saw the movie when I was a kid and liked it, and I do wonder if I'll have a different outlook on it 30 years later. And that's especially in comparing it to the last few years of what we've gotten.
2. A FEW GOOD MEN is one of those films that looks old and dated at first glance but reveals a deceptive brilliance as it unfolds. Directing the rhythm of a Sorkin script is a hefty task and it was thrilling to see Reiner pull it off without betraying a brushstroke. AMERICAN PRESIDENT is the only other film I've seen from him and I will rectify that error soon.

Going out the way he did, especially at his age, is awful and senseless. I hope he and his wife rest in peace.
  - Please check out Stand By Me
3. Aside from his accomplishments as a director I really love his role as Jordan's dad in The Wolf of Wall Street. Exceptionally funny character in a movie that's already funny on its own merit.
4. The Body is literally one of my favorite stories of all time and Rob Reiner captured all of its nuances and heartache to the T. I’m so devastated by this news. He was just a brilliant craftsman and a brilliant person with a generous generous heart. I don’t know anything about his personal life but he made movies that brought so much joy into my life. Thank you and rest in peace Mr. & Mrs. Reiner 😢
5. This one hits hard because of in addition to how much I love his films themselves, I carry with me what he said with them, and the kind of values he decided to act with in his own life.  

A couple of people on social media explained this better than I could:

> Rob Reiner was a good guy. He made a lot of movies about good guys, in worlds where not everyone was good and you didn’t have to be good. He made movies about people trying. He was angry, as many hopeful people are. He knew the world could be better and he wanted it to be. His death is devastating. 

-[Jessica Ellis, over on bsky](https://bsky.app/profile/baddestmamajama.bsky.social/post/3m7yq7urfd222)

> Rob Reiner was one of the key people behind the effort to go to court to overturn California's anti-marriage equality Proposition 8 when it passed in 2008. 
> Working with Chad Griffin, they founded the American Foundation for Equal Rights and got Ted Olson and David Boies to team up to fight Prop 8. 

-[Chris Geider, over on bsky](https://bsky.app/profile/chrisgeidner.bsky.social/post/3m7yqexg62k2o)
6. Oh shit! One of the best. I'm old enough to remember wondering if the guy from All in the Family could really be the same guy who made Princess Bride, When Harry Met Sally and (one of the two best comedies of all time) Spinal Tap! RIP Rob.

## 2. The Voice of Hind Rajab (2025),  A Devastating Testament to an Ongoing Tragedy.

**Score:** 22
**Body:** There are movies based on true events, movies showing true events, and then there are rare films like The Voice of Hind Rajab, a film that portrays not only real events but current ones, unfolding as the world watches.



We spend a few harrowing hours inside a Palestinian Red Crescent call centre as they receive a call from six-year-old Hind Rajab, trapped in a car that has just been shot at, leaving everyone but her dead inside. The operators desperately try to help by sending an ambulance. While the call centre workers are portrayed by actors, the voice you hear is real. It is Hind herself, telling us she's surrounded by corpses, that she's being shot at, pleading for help that may never come.



The film succeeds devastatingly in showing the frustration and soul-destroying effect this has on the operators. The cinematography moves urgently between the people, capturing the frantic desperation of the moment. Near the end, the movie gradually introduces the real individuals, and you realize the actors did a remarkable job portraying these actual people who lived through this nightmare.



The Voice of Hind Rajab is a masterpiece that demands to be seen, not just as cinema, but as a document of our times, a testament to a tragedy we cannot look away from.



This is essential viewing.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve been working up the courage to watch it. I’ve got a pretty strong stomach for most films but this one sounds tougher than most. I agree with your take that it merits watching not only as a film but as a present day tragedy that we must pay attention to.

I’ve been working up the courage to watch it. I’ve got a pretty strong stomach for most films but this one sounds tougher than most. I agree with your take that it merits watching not only as a film but as a present day tragedy that we must pay attention to.
2. The very existence of this film brings up some interesting ethical/artistic dilemmas.  
Having actors crying and performing trauma alongside realtime audio of a young girl experiences her final, terrifying hours of life on earth could feel exploitative at worst... but at best it highlights the artifice of narrative form, and its utter powerlessness to capture the reality of the horrors unfolding currently while you watch the film.

I'll be honest, I was kind of against the film before I even saw it. But I think Kouther Ben Hania made some very interesting structural and artistic decisions in the way she delineated artifice and verisimilitude. In the end, the film won me over.

On the other hand, I still have to wonder about the dramaturgy. These are all real people, dealing with their real traumas here. And now they are characters in a film that must keep up the tension for 90 minutes. So the man doing the coordinating is headstrong and fighting with the operators every step of the way (at one point even being told "It's because of people like you that we are occupied"). How does he feel about his depiction in the film? (I actually have some connection to some of the people involved in this story... and it seems to me the actual answer is "right now... they haven't had the capacity to consider the film much at all". But who knows in the long run?)

A complicated, troubling film... in its form and its content. But I'm glad I watched it, and I'd encourage others to do so, just because it offers much to chew on.

## 3. I’m genuinely confused by the new Animal Farm movie.

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Ive just watched the trailer for the first time and I’m honestly baffled.

What is this? How did this happen? Am I losing my mind?

Looking at the creative team makes this even more confusing.

Andy Serkis is directing. Seth Rogen is starring. The cast includes Kieran Culkin and Woody Harrelson.

Andy Serkis, known for Gollum and Caesar, is someone I genuinely consider an artist.

Seth Rogen is an openly liberal, pot-smoking comedian who has mocked religion multiple times in his own work. He also executive produced The Boys, which is about as anti-authoritarian and cynical as mainstream TV gets.

Woody Harrelson is another actor I consider a serious artist.

Even Glenn Close just released a film that did not exactly portray religion favorably.

So these are not naïve people. These are not people without taste, politics, or an understanding of subtext.

Which is why the trailer completely floored me.

My first instinct was honestly that it was a misdirect. Make the trailer overly saccharine and poppy, lure people in, then hit them with the darker political and philosophical messaging of the book. That would actually be kind of clever, and totally in character for some of the people involved.

But then I learned the film was purchased by a religious studio. And the way it is being presented makes it clear this is not some bait-and-switch. The message appears to have been softened or outright diluted.

So I am left with one big question.

Why?

Why did all of these very talented, very media-savvy, very openly political people agree to this version of the adaptation? How does the ball get dropped this badly?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think Animal Farm probably got purchased by Angel Studios because they would freakin do it. I don't think that means it's going to have a faith based message.

I think this will very much be a Seth Rogan project. Funny, weird, kind of unfocused, all right. I don't think it's a cynical cash in or some kind of weird agenda play.

I'd rather take my kids to see this than Bad Guys 2.
2. I'm not optimistic either, but it's common enough for trailers to not reflect the real tone of a film, and maybe that's what happened here - the marketing department dropped the ball
3. The movie was sold to that Mormon based studio not based on any held beliefs but because literally no other studio has shown interest in buying it since it premiered 6 months ago. There's no chance in hell they are going to shoot new material for anything faith-based to go into the movie, though there is the possibility that cuts will be made. 

The bigger concern away from who picked up the movie is WHY no other distributor would pick it up. Whatever is wrong with this movie was wrong with it in 2022 when production started.

## 4. Casual Discussion Thread (December 16, 2025)

**Score:** 1
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)
* [Twitter](https://twitter.com/truefilmreddit)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Isnt 90% of what people call good acting, just the voice?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I have notice that almost exclusively what people mean when they say an actor is a good actor its the voice. the actor may look stiff as a board and with no facial expression at all but if what comes out of their mouth sounds cool and real then its good acting.

some other actors, may gesticulate, cry and move perfectly as if they were not really acting but if they have a lame voice then it’s considered bad acting.

To go even further, here in Mexico there are some interpetations that are considered very good becuse the dubbing makes them sound (and look) way better than in their original languages. The “kal-el, no!” Of wonder woman in the justice league movie Was never a joke here because the dub made her sound way better

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Maybe it would be better with examples?

I can thing of counter example- Hayden Christensen in Revange of the Sith absolutely delivers in silent scenes but struggle with anything to be said (granted, I don't know who could save those cheesy lies, but still)
2. No, not at all. In fact a poor actor relies solely on their voice. Good acting comes from behavior and the body. All of what you’re saying is the body. I would consider reading books on acting such as The Actors Art And Craft by William Esper or On Acting by Sanford Meisner.
3. No, otherwise everyone could be a good actor with the right dubbing, which is not the case. Facial expressions, eye movement, body gestures, all play a part in acting.  

Look at the infamous “oh hi Mark” scene in The Room without audio, and you’ll immediately be able to see that it’s a poor performance.
4. No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
No No No No No No No No
5. The Gal Gadot scene you’re referencing is not mocked because of just the voice/pronunciation. It’s mocked because the acting is all around bad. If that line was said with the exact same awkward pronunciation, but even a hint of emotion that was in line with the scene would work. She’s watching her good friend turn evil and is pleading with them to stop. She says this with the same emotion someone would use when telling their kid for the 10000th they can’t have a cookie.

Acting can be helped and hindered by many things. A bad edit can make an actor look bad even if they’d find a good job. A good actor can be hurt by a bad director. A bad actor can be helped with a Laissez-faire filming style. 

I personally think Gena Rowlands in “A Woman Under The Influence” is one of the greatest acting performances ever to be put on film. I’d suggest watching that and looking for those the subtle things she does to transform it from a good acting to great acting.

I also like to recommend the movie Dogville, it’s an interesting movie that forces you to watch the acting. There’s nothing else to pay attention to.
6. Amazing actors can communicate without uttering a single word.

Some viewers have difficulty distinguishing between the script and the performance when it comes to actors.

Many big Oscar acting moments are really dramatic monologues. Was the use of the voice the compelling part of the performance or the words that were spoken?

Similarly, some people say they think certain actors are "good" actors, but when pressed, they like the characters the actors play or simply find the person attractive.

A visually unattractive actor with a golden voice and a bad script is unlikely to receive accolades for their acting.
7. I think that Gal Gadot example felt like such bad acting because both the physical performance (face and body) and the voice performance felt unemotional. But the main flaw of the physical performance was its *blankness*. It’s not as if she was smiling or making a disgusted face or anything. So fixing the voice performance could do a lot to reduce how out of place it felt.

I’m curious about your examples of actors who “look stiff as a board and with no facial expression at all” but who sound cool and real and it’s seen as a good performance. I wonder if you’re primarily talking about characters where their cool stoicism is part of the appeal.

And whether those are considered good performances in the way that people appreciate a honed and complex skill. Or whether they’re considered good performances more in the sense that they look cool and are fun to watch.
8. Ha ha, never. 

A great actor knows his canvas. How to perform in close-up or at a distance, how to find his light, how to USE the light and how it affects how his expressions are read. She knows when to ignore directions in the script and try something completely different, something that brings humanity and individuality to a character versus the expectations of genre. She does not merely emote, she invites the audience to empathize with her so strongly that they see the emotions the character doesn’t allow to show on her face. He can have not a single line of dialogue, but take you through a complex line of thought, from realization, reaction, planning and resignation, all within a few seconds. She performs just as well in prestige genres as big-budget genre fare, although she knows that it calls for different tools. He lifts up his co-performers and elevates their performances without ever sacrificing his own. She knows how to cultivate good chemistry with other actors, but not always the same chemistry. He is capable of performing comedy and tragedy excellently, even with the same scene. She never loses respect for her audience or herself, never phones it in or behaves so badly on set it affects the other filmmakers. He doesn’t take advantage of his position or respect to prey on others and destabilize the film. She can act with literally any part of her body, even if it is only a hand in close up. He can act even while wearing a mask for the entire performance. 

A great actor can do an entire performance in mime alone.

---
